eviction.court_process_summary_rule:
{"statutes":"Minn. Stat. §§ 504B.331, 504B.335, 504B.301","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"14-day notice to pay or vacate (HF 4770 of 2024 extended from 3 to 14 days effective 2024)","holdover_tenant":"3-month notice to terminate for year-to-year tenancy; 1-month notice for month-to-month","lease_violation":"14-day notice to cure or vacate for noncompliance","no_cause":"Statewide just cause not required; Minneapolis and St. Paul require just cause for covered units"},"full_text":"Minn. Stat. § 504B.331. Notice to vacate. (a) A landlord may bring an eviction action if the tenant fails to pay rent when due. The landlord shall give the tenant 14 days' written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate the premises. (b) If the tenant fails to pay rent within the 14-day period, the landlord may bring an eviction action.
Minn. Stat. § 504B.335. Eviction action. The landlord shall bring an eviction action in conciliation court or district court. The court shall hold a hearing within 7 to 10 days after the complaint is served. If the court finds that the tenant owes rent or has violated a material term of the lease, the court may issue an order for restitution of the premises.
Minneapolis Just Cause Eviction (Minneapolis Code of Ordinances, Title 16, Chapter 244): Landlords must have just cause to terminate a tenancy for covered units. Just cause includes nonpayment of rent, material breach, nuisance, illegal activity, owner move-in, demolition, and substantial rehabilitation.
St. Paul Just Cause Eviction (St. Paul Legislative Code, Chapter 183): Similar just cause requirements apply to covered residential units in St. Paul."}

{"statutes":"Minn. Stat. § 504B.178","full_text":"Minn. Stat. § 504B.178. Security deposit.
Subdivision 1. Maximum amount. A landlord may not charge a security deposit that exceeds an amount equal to 2 months' rent. A landlord may permit a tenant to pay the security deposit in installments.
Subdivision 2. Receipt. The landlord shall give the tenant a written receipt for any security deposit.
Subdivision 3. Condition list. The landlord shall provide the tenant with a written inventory of the condition of the premises, including all furnishings and appliances, within 5 days after the tenant takes possession. The tenant may add to or dispute the inventory.
Subdivision 4. Interest. The landlord shall pay interest on the security deposit at the rate of 1% per year or at the rate determined by the Commissioner of Commerce, whichever is greater. The interest is payable to the tenant within 21 days after the end of each year of the tenancy.
Subdivision 5. Return of deposit. Within 21 days after the termination of the tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any lawful deductions. The landlord shall provide an itemized statement of any deductions with a list of the deductions and the balance due.
Subdivision 6. Penalties. If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 21 days, the landlord is liable to the tenant for: (a) The full amount of the security deposit; (b) Treble damages if the failure was willful and malicious; (c) Reasonable attorney fees."}
extractedQuotes: Maximum amount: 2 months' rent

Subdivision 5. Return of deposit. Within 21 days after the termination of the tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any lawful deductions. The landlord shall provide an itemized statement of any deductions with a list of the deductions and the balance due.
Subdivision 6. Penalties. If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 21 days, the landlord is liable to the tenant for: (a) The full amount of the security deposit; (b) Treble damages if the failure was willful and malicious; (c) Reasonable attorney fees."}

sources.title:
{"statutes":"Minn. Stat. §§ 504B.331, 504B.335, 504B.301","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"14-day notice to pay or vacate (HF 4770 of 2024 extended from 3 to 14 days effective 2024)","holdover_tenant":"3-month notice to terminate for year-to-year tenancy; 1-month notice for month-to-month","lease_violation":"14-day notice to cure or vacate for noncompliance","no_cause":"Statewide just cause not required; Minneapolis and St. Paul require just cause for covered units"},"full_text":"Minn. Stat. § 504B.331. Notice to vacate. (a) A landlord may bring an eviction action if the tenant fails to pay rent when due. The landlord shall give the tenant 14 days' written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate the premises. (b) If the tenant fails to pay rent within the 14-day period, the landlord may bring an eviction action.
Minn. Stat. § 504B.335. Eviction action. The landlord shall bring an eviction action in conciliation court or district court. The court shall hold a hearing within 7 to 10 days after the complaint is served. If the court finds that the tenant owes rent or has violated a material term of the lease, the court may issue an order for restitution of the premises.
Minneapolis Just Cause Eviction (Minneapolis Code of Ordinances, Title 16, Chapter 244): Landlords must have just cause to terminate a tenancy for covered units. Just cause includes nonpayment of rent, material breach, nuisance, illegal activity, owner move-in, demolition, and substantial rehabilitation.
St. Paul Just Cause Eviction (St. Paul Legislative Code, Chapter 183): Similar just cause requirements apply to covered residential units in St. Paul."}

Stat. §§ 504B.331, 504B.335, 504B.301","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"14-day notice to pay or vacate (HF 4770 of 2024 extended from 3 to 14 days effective 2024)","holdover_tenant":"3-month notice to terminate for year-to-year tenancy; 1-month notice for month-to-month","lease_violation":"14-day notice to cure or vacate for noncompliance","no_cause":"Statewide just cause not required; Minneapolis and St. Paul require just cause for covered units"},"full_text":"Minn. Stat. § 504B.331. Notice to vacate. (a) A landlord may bring an eviction action if the tenant fails to pay rent when due. The landlord shall give the tenant 14 days' written notice requiring the tenant to pay rent or vacate the premises. (b) If the tenant fails to pay rent within the 14-day period, the landlord may bring an eviction action.
Minn. Stat. § 504B.335. Eviction action. The landlord shall bring an eviction action in conciliation court or district court. The court shall hold a hearing within 7 to 10 days after the complaint is served. If the court finds that the tenant owes rent or has violated a material term of the lease, the court may issue an order for restitution of the premises.
Minneapolis Just Cause Eviction (Minneapolis Code of Ordinances, Title 16, Chapter 244): Landlords must have just cause to terminate a tenancy for covered units. Just cause includes nonpayment of rent, material breach, nuisance, illegal activity, owner move-in, demolition, and substantial rehabilitation.
St. Paul Just Cause Eviction (St. Paul Legislative Code, Chapter 183): Similar just cause requirements apply to covered residential units in St. Paul."}

sources.title:
{"statutes":"Minn. Stat. § 504B.211","full_text":"Minn. Stat. § 504B.211. Landlord's right to enter the premises.
Subdivision 1. Lawful entry. The landlord may enter the premises only: (a) At a reasonable time; (b) In a reasonable manner; (c) After making a reasonable effort to notify the tenant of the intended entry. Reasonable notice is presumed to be at least 24 hours before the entry.
Subdivision 2. Permissible purposes. The landlord may enter the premises for the following purposes: (a) To inspect the premises; (b) To make ordinary, necessary, or agreed repairs, alterations, or improvements; (c) To supply necessary or agreed services; (d) To exhibit the premises to prospective or actual purchasers, mortgagees, tenants, workers, or contractors; (e) If the tenant is absent and the landlord reasonably believes that entry is necessary to protect the premises or property.
Subdivision 3. Emergency. The landlord may enter without notice in an emergency.
Subdivision 4. Tenant remedies. If the landlord makes an unlawful entry or makes a lawful entry in an unreasonable manner, the tenant may recover actual damages or $500, whichever is greater, plus reasonable attorney fees."}

sources.title: {"statutes":"Minn. Stat. §§ 504B.161, 504B.221","full_text":"Minn. Stat. § 504B.161. Landlord's duties. The landlord shall: (a) Comply with all applicable building and housing code requirements that materially affect health and safety; (b) Make all repairs and do whatever is necessary to keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition; (c) Keep all common areas in a safe and sanitary condition; (d) Maintain in good working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, and air conditioning fixtures and appliances; (e) Provide and maintain appropriate garbage containers and arrange for garbage removal if the landlord is responsible for removal; (f) Supply running water and reasonable amounts of hot water at all times and reasonable heat unless the heat is generated by a system under the tenant's exclusive control.\nMinn. Stat. § 504B.221. Implied warranty of habitability. In every residential tenancy, there is an implied warranty that the premises are and will remain fit for human habitation. This warranty may not be waived. If the landlord violates this warranty, the tenant may: (a) Give written notice specifying the violation; (b) If the landlord fails to remedy within a reasonable time, the tenant may: (i) Withhold rent and bring a proceeding to determine the reasonable rental value; (ii) Recover damages; (iii) Terminate the lease; (iv) In Minneapolis and St. Paul, repair and deduct."}
